coredata.dc:title: Replacement of Pd nanoparticles: Hydrogenation promoted by frustrated Lewis acid-base pairs in carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Bingqing
Liu, Guangsheng
Deng, Xiaoyu
Deng, Zirong
Lin, Wei
Li, Zhaohui
coredata.dc:description: Due to the scarcity of the noble metals and their high price, the use of carbon-based materials to replace the noble metal catalysts has attracted extensive current research interest. Herein, we reported that carbon quantum dots (CQDs) can be used to replace Pd nanoparticles in hydrogenation reactions due to the presence of the frustrated Lewis acid-base pairs (FLPs), as demonstrated experimentally and supported theoretically. By coupling the hydrogenation of imines over CQDs with ZnIn2S4-based photocatalytic dehydrogenation of alcohols, N-alkylation of amines with alcohols have been successfully realized over CQDs/ZnIn2S4, with performance comparable to that observed over Pd/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposite, demonstrating the possibility of using CQDs to substitute Pd nanoparticles in hydrogenation of imines. This work shows enormous potential of employing the CQDs in chemical reactions to reduce the current dependence on noble metal-based catalysts.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Frustrated Lewis acid-base pairs
N-alkylation reactions
Hydrogenation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) XRD pattern of 3.0 wt% CQDs/ZnIn2S4 and bare ZnIn2S4. (B) XPS spectra of 3.0 wt% CQDs/ZnIn2S4 in C 1s region. (C) XPS spectra of 3.0 wt% CQDs/ZnIn2S4 in O 1s region. (D) TEM image of 3.0 wt% CQDs/ZnIn2S4. (E) HRTEM image of 3.0 wt% CQDs/ZnIn2S4, the inset represents an enlarged HRTEM image of 3.0 wt% CQDs/ZnIn2S4. (F) UV–vis spectra of bare ZnIn2S4 and 3.0 wt% CQDs/ZnIn2S4.
Changes of the amount of the products and aniline in light initiated N-alkylation reaction over 3.0 wt% CQDs/ZnIn2S4.

(A) Structures of CQDs model. (B) Structures of ZnIn2S4 model. (C) The charge density of CQDs/ZnIn2S4, the pink and cyan surfaces represent the accumulation and depletion of charge, respectively, with isovalue of 0.02 e/Å3. (D) Structures of CQDs/ZnIn2S4 after H adsorbed.
The proposed mechanism of N-alkylation of aniline with benzyl alcohol over CQDs/ZnIn2S4.
Light initiated N-alkylation of aniline with benzyl alcohol in different conditions.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Reaction conditions: 10 mg catalyst, 0.2 mmol aniline, N2, 2 mL solvent, irradiated for 18 h.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: d
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The photocatalyst was removed after 18 h of irradiation.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Investigation on the substrate scopes of N-alkylation of amines with alcohols over 3.0 wt% CQDs/ZnIn2S4 under irradiation.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Reaction conditions: 0.2 mmol amine, 10 mg 3.0 wt% CQDs/ZnIn2S4, N2, 2 mL solvent.

head.ce:title: Replacement of Pd nanoparticles: Hydrogenation promoted by frustrated Lewis acid-base pairs in carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Institute of Photocatalysis, State Key Laboratory of Photocatalysis on Energy and Environment, College of Chemistry, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou 350116, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Photocatalysis on Energy and Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Institute of Photocatalysis, State Key Laboratory of Photocatalysis on Energy and Environment, College of Chemistry, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou 350116, People’s Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Due to the scarcity of the noble metals and their high price, the use of carbon-based materials to replace the noble metal catalysts has attracted extensive current research interest. Herein, we reported that carbon quantum dots (CQDs) can be used to replace Pd nanoparticles in hydrogenation reactions due to the presence of the frustrated Lewis acid-base pairs (FLPs), as demonstrated experimentally and supported theoretically. By coupling the hydrogenation of imines over CQDs with ZnIn2S4-based photocatalytic dehydrogenation of alcohols, N-alkylation of amines with alcohols have been successfully realized over CQDs/ZnIn2S4, with performance comparable to that observed over Pd/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposite, demonstrating the possibility of using CQDs to substitute Pd nanoparticles in hydrogenation of imines. This work shows enormous potential of employing the CQDs in chemical reactions to reduce the current dependence on noble metal-based catalysts.

ce:section.ce:para: Catalysis plays a crucial role in many chemical reactions [1–3]. Although most of the catalysts currently used are based on metals, metal-free catalysts, in particular, the carbon materials, have attracted enormous current research interest in heterogeneous catalysis due to their interesting properties as well as their abundance [4,5]. A variety of carbon allotropes, such as graphene, graphene oxide, carbon nanotubes, have already been demonstrated to be active in some catalytic reactions, which previously can only be realized over metal-based catalysts [6–8]. Defects and diversified chemical functionalities like carbon vacancies, dopants and other functional groups, which either casually or purposely be introduced into these carbon materials, are found to be the catalytic active sites [9]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a new emerging carbon material, share similar hexagonal arrangement of sp2 carbons and surface functional groups with other carbon allotropes [10]. It is therefore anticipated that CQDs can also be catalytic active in some reactions as other carbon allotropes. In addition, the small dimension of the CQDs may endow it with more exposed catalytic active sites and should be beneficial for the catalysis [11]. Actually, there are already some reports on using CQDs for organic systheses, such as CC coupling reactions and aerobic oxidations [12–14]. Although only a few examples have been reported in this area, these limited studies demonstrate the enormous potential of employing CQDs to replace some metal-based catalysts in chemical reactions and requires more explorations.

Hydrogenation is a kind of important reaction in chemical industry, in which Pd nanoparticles play an important role due to their strong ability to activate H2 and the formation of Pd-H, the active species for hydrogenation reaction [15–17]. Recent studies have revealed that in addition to Pd nanoparticles, frustrated Lewis acid-base pairs (FLPs) can also promote the dissociation of H2 and are active for the hydrogenation [18–21]. For example, some graphene-based materials which contain carbon vacancies and oxygenated functional groups acting as Lewis acid and base respectively, have been demonstrated to be active in the hydrogenation of CC bonds [22].

Direct N-alkylation reactions using alcohols as alkylating agents is a fascinating strategy for the synthesis of N-alkyl amines due to its high atom efficiency [23–26]. Based on the well developed “hydrogen auto-transfer” mechanism for N-alkylation of amines with alcohols, which follows three tandem processes, ie, dehydrogenation of alcohols to aldehydes, condensation between amines and aldehydes to form imines and the hydrogenation of imines to N-alkyl amines, a series of Pd/photocatalysts like Pd/ZnIn2S4, Pd/TiO2, Pd@MIL-100(Fe) as well as AuPd@MIL-100(Fe), have been used for visible light initiated N-alkylation of amines with alcohols [27–30].

Stimulated by the previous discoveries that the presence of FLPs in graphene-based materials can promote the hydrogenation of CC bonds and CQDs share common structural characteristics with other carbon allotropes, and considering that Pd/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposites exhibit excellent performance for visible light initiated N-alkylation of amines by alcohols, in this manuscript, we investigated the performance of the CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposites for the same reaction to see the possibility of replacing the expensive Pd nanoparticles with cheaper CQDs. CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposites were obtained via a simple solvothermally assisted self-assembly of ZnIn2S4 in the presence of CQDs. It was found that the as-obtained CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposites exhibited excellent performance for visible light initiated N-alkylation of amines by alcohols, with a performance comparable to that over Pd/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposites. The observation of the activity for N-alkylation of amines over CQDs/ZnIn2S4 can be ascribed to the successful coupling of ZnIn2S4-based photocatalytic dehydrogenation of alcohols with hydrogenation of imine over CQDs. Both the theoretical calculation and the experimental results suggested that FLPs in CQDs can act similarly as Pd for hydrogenation of imines to realize an overall N-alkylation of amines with alcohols over CQDs/ZnIn2S4. This work demonstrates the possibility of using CQDs to substitute Pd nanoparticles in hydrogenation reactions and shows enormous potential of employing the CQDs-based materials in reactions to reduce the current dependence on noble metal-based catalysts.

The 3.0 wt% CQDs/ZnIn2S4 was obtained via solvothermally assisted self-assembly of ZnIn2S4 in the presence of CQDs. The CQDs (12.7 mg), which was obtained from citric acid according to previous literature [31], diffused in mixed solution containing ethylene glycol (30 mL) and N, N-Dimethylformamide (30 mL). Then InCl3·4H2O (2.0 mmol, 0.586 g) and ZnCl2 (1.0 mmol, 0.136 g) were added to the mixed solution under vigorous stirring. Thioacetamide (4.0 mmol, 0.3 g) was added to the above solution and the mixed solution transferred to Teflon liner and sealed in stainless steel autoclave, reacted at 180 °C for 18 h. Different amount of CQDs in CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposites were denoted as x wt% CQDs/ZnIn2S4 (x = 1.0, 3.0, 5.0).

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the samples were performed on D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, Germany) with Cu Kα (λ = 1.5406 Å) radiation at 40 mA and 40 kV. XRD patterns were scanned from 10 to 70° (2θ) with step size at 0.05°. The FT-IR measurements were performed on Nicolet IS50 FT-IR spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were performed on PHI Quantum 2000 XPS system (PHI, USA) with a charge neutralizer and a monochromatic Al Ka source. All the binding energies were referenced to the C1s peak at 284.6 eV of the surface adventitious carbon. The morphology of the samples was observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (JSM-6700F) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). High resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were obtained from JEOL model JEM 2010 EX instrument with an accelerating voltage at 200 kV. UV − visible diffuse reflectance spectra (UV-DRS, Cary 500) of the samples were performed and the BaSO4 acted as reflectance. H2-TPD measurement were performed with the Auto-Chem II 2920 station. The in-situ IR measurements were carried out on a Nicolet IS50 FT-IR spectrometer equipped with an in situ diffuse reflectance cell (Harrick).

The N-alkylation reactions were performed in sealed reaction tube under 5W LED lamp (PCX50A, Beijing Perfectlight Technology Co.) irradiation. For the N-alkylation of aniline with benzyl alcohol, the 10 mg photocatalyst and aniline (0.2 mmol) were dispersed in excess benzyl alcohol. The above solution was saturated with N2 to remove the dissolved O2 before the reaction. Then, the reaction was performed under 5W LED irradiation. After the reaction, the suspension filtered through a porous membrane and the filtrate was analyzed by GC-FID (GC-2014, Shimadzu) with HP-5 capillary column equipped and GC–MS (7890B -5977A, Agilent).

For the hydrogenation of N-benzylideneaniline (3a) to targeted N-benzylaniline (3b) using molecular hydrogen, 10 mg of 3.0 wt% CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposite and N-benzylideneaniline (0.2 mmol) was suspended in a mixed solvent containing CH2Cl2 and benzyl alcohol with a volume ratio of 1:5, which was purged with H2 (1 atm) to remove any dissolved O2. The reaction system was irradiated with a 5 W LED. After the reaction, the suspension was filtered and the filtrate was analyzed by the GC.

The CQDs/ZnIn2S4 computational models were built up based on the structural information obtained in our experiments and the literature. The structure of CQDs is shown in Fig. 3A, where two COO− groups, two COOH groups, and three CO groups are considered in conjugated carbon rings. The length of CQDs is ~1 nm. As the CQDs size is large, a 5 × 5 supercell and 7 atomic layers along the [0 0 1] projection was used to mimic the ZnIn2S4 nanosheets. The structures of sulfur-vacancy ZnIn2S4 were obtained by removing one or two surface sulfur atoms following the geometric optimization. When two hydrogen atoms were added on CQDs/ZnIn2S4, one of them was bonded to COO− and the other was placed on multiple sites nearby carbon atoms of CQDs. The most stable adsorption was usually found to be the carbon bonded with COO− group with the distance of H---H be ~2 Angstrom. All the electronic structure calculations were performed via the VASP 5.4 package [32,33]. The PBE exchange-correlation functional was used to represent the exchange and correlation interactions, while electron − ion interactions were mimicked using projector-augmented wave (PAW) potentials [34–36]. The plane wave cutoff energy was set as 400 eV, and a 2 × 2 × 1 Monkhorst-Pack mesh was used for k-sampling of the Brillouin zone. The conventional energy of 10−4 eV and force convergence criteria of 0.02 eV/Å was applied for the ionic relaxations. A 1.2 nm vacuum layer was added to avoid the electronic interactions between vertically neighboring cells.

CQDs/ZnIn2S4 were obtained via self-assembly of ZnIn2S4 in the presence of CQDs under solvothermal treatment. The as-obtained product (take 3.0 wt% CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposite for example) shows similar XRD pattern to bare hexagonal ZnIn2S4, indicating that hexagonal ZnIn2S4 has been successfully formed in the presence of CQDs (Fig. 1 A) [37,38]. Although the diffraction pattern belongs to CQDs is not found, two peaks at 1566 cm−1 and 1398 cm−1, assignable to asymmetric and symmetric stretches of the carboxylate groups of the CQDs, are observed in the FT-IR spectrum of 3.0 wt% CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposite (Fig. S1) [39]. The XPS spectra in C 1s region of 3.0 wt% CQDs/ZnIn2S4 exhibits three peaks at 287.9 eV, 286.1 eV and 284.6 eV, assignable to carboxylate groups, hydroxyl carbon and sp2 hybridized carbon respectively, further confirming the presence of CQDs in CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposite (Fig. 1B) [40]. Correspondingly, two peaks at 532.7 eV and 531.9 eV, assignable to O in the hydroxyl groups and carboxylate, are also observed in the XPS spectra in O 1 s region (Fig. 1C) [41]. As compared with bare ZnIn2S4, a shifts toward higher binding energy for both Zn2+ 2p and In3+ 3d was observed over 3.0 wt% CQDs/ZnIn2S4, suggesting that there is strong interaction between CQDs and ZnIn2S4 (Fig. S2). Flower-like microspheres ca. 2.5 μm with numerous of interwoven ultrathin nanosheets are observed from the TEM and SEM images of 3.0 wt% CQDs/ZnIn2S4 (Fig. 1D, Fig. S3). The HRTEM shows distinct lattice fringes of 0.22 nm and 0.32 nm, assignable to (1 0 0) plane of graphite phase CQDs and (0 0 2) plane of hexagonal ZnIn2S4 (Fig. 1E) [42]. All these characterizations demonstrated that CQDs deposited on ZnIn2S4 microspheres has been successfully obtained via the solvothermal treatment. As compared with that of bare ZnIn2S4, the UV–vis DRS of CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposite does not show obvious change in 400–750 nm region (Fig. 1F).

The performance of the as-obtained CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposites for the N-alkylation reaction was investigated. The reaction between aniline and benzyl alcohol was selected as the model reaction, which was initially performed in benzyl alcohol acting both as reactant and solvent, over 1.0 wt% CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposite under visible light. As that previously observed over Pd/ZnIn2S4, the targeted product N-benzylaniline (3b) was observed, with the benzaldehyde and N-benzylideneaniline (3a) as the by-products (Table 1 , entry 1). 36% of aniline was converted with a yield of 24% to targeted 3b after irradiated for 18 h. Aniline was hardly converted in absence of 1.0 wt% CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposite or in dark (Table 1, entries 2–3). While 1.0 wt% CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposite was filtered after 18 h of irradiation, no further reaction proceeded (Table 1, entry 4). These results indicated that the N-alkylation reaction was actually induced by 1.0 wt% CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposite.

CQDs plays an important role in this reaction. As observed previously, bare ZnIn2S4 showed inferior performance in this reaction. Only 8% of aniline was converted under similar condition (Table 1, entry 5). Instead of targeted 3b, the main product obtained over irradiated ZnIn2S4 was 3a (5%). The amount of CQDs in CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposites also influences the performance. Elevating the loading of CQDs from 1.0 wt% to 3.0 wt% led to an increase in both the conversion of aniline (54%) and the yield to targeted 3b (46%). However, a further increase of the amount of CQDs to 5.0 wt% resulted in a slightly decreased conversion of aniline (48%) and a lower yield (37%) to targeted 3b, probably ascribed to a shading effect by CQDs (Table 1, entries 6–7).

Since CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposite containing 3.0 wt% CQDs exhibited the optimum activity, 3.0 wt% CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposite was chosen to study the influence of the solvents on the reaction (Table 1, entries 8–11). Mixed solvents containing benzyl alcohol with H2O, CH3CN, toluene and CH2Cl2 respectively, with volume ratio to 1:1, were applied in the N-alkylation reaction over 3.0 wt% CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposite. Among these solvents screened, mixed solvent containing benzyl alcohol with CH2Cl2 in 1:1 vol ratio exhibited the highest activity with conversion ratio of aniline at 66% and yield of 51% to the targeted 3b under irradiation for 18 h. For further optimization, reactions carried out in the reaction medium with benzyl alcohol and CH2Cl2 in different volume ratio was also investigated (Table 1, entries 12–14). An optimum conversion of aniline (88%) and yield of the targeted 3b (77%) was obtained in mixed solvent containing CH2Cl2 and benzyl alcohol with volume ratio to 1:5 after irradiated for 18 h (Table 1, entry 13). Such a catalytic activity for the N-alkylation reaction over 3.0 wt% CQDs/ZnIn2S4 is comparable to that reported previously over Pd/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposite, in which 82% of aniline was converted with a yield of the targeted 3b at 73%, indicating the feasibility of using CQDs to substitute Pd nanoparticles in the N-alkylation of amines. Extending the irradiation time to 24 h gave a complete conversion of aniline (99%) with yield of the targeted 3b at 97% (Table 1, entry 15).

The time-dependent N-alkylation of aniline with benzyl alcohol in CH2Cl2 and benzyl alcohol (1:5) over 3.0 wt% CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposite showed that the amount of the targeted 3b increased, while that of 3a increased in the first 3 h and then decreased, indicating that 3a as an intermediate was transformed to the targeted 3b during reaction process (Fig. 2
). Although the amount of benzaldehyde in the reaction system also increased with the irradiation time, its amount was lower than that obtained in a reaction system in absence of aniline, ie, the dehydrogenation of benzyl alcohol (Fig. S4). This indicates that in the presence of aniline, part of the as-formed benzaldehyde can react with the aniline to produce intermediate 3a, which can be hydrogenated to form targeted 3b. This observation is similar to our previous studies on Pd@MIL-100(Fe) and Pd/ZnIn2S4 for the N-alkylation reaction [27,29], suggesting the replacement of Pd with CQDs in Pd/ZnIn2S4 also led to N-alkylated product of aniline.

From the above time-dependent reaction, it is obvious that 3a is the intermediate to produce the targeted 3b, therefore the transformation of 3a to 3b was also studied under different conditions. It was found that 39% of N-benzylideneaniline (3a) was hydrogenated to targeted 3b after irradiated for 6 h. Prolonging the reaction time to 18 h led to a complete conversion of 3a to 3b. On the contrary, almost negligible 3b was obtained over bare ZnIn2S4 under otherwise similar conditions, indicating that CQDs is indispensable for the hydrogenation of 3a to 3b. When CQDs/ZnIn2S4 was replaced by CQDs/Al2O3 nanocomposite or the reaction was carried out over 3.0 wt% CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposite without irradiations, no hydrogenation of 3a occurred, indicating that the hydrogenation of 3a can only be realized over electron-rich CQDs obtained by accepting the photo-generated electrons from irradiated ZnIn2S4. The hydrogenation of 3a to targeted 3b also occurred over irradiated 3.0 wt% CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposite when H2 was used as the hydrogen source, although the conversion ratio was lower than that in benzyl alcohol, indicating the dissociation of H2 for the hydrogenation of the imine can be promoted by irradiated CQDs/ZnIn2S4. Previous studies revealed that FLPs can promote the dissociation of H2 via polarizing H2 to form H+ and H--like species for hydrogenation reactions [18]. In particular, the vacancies and oxygenated functional groups in RGO can act as Lewis acid and base respectively, which enable the hydrogenation of CC bonds to be realized [6]. To investigate whether a similar mechanism occurs over the current system, theoretical calculations were also carried out on CQDs/ZnIn2S4.

As shown in Fig. 3C, CQDs/ZnIn2S4 with the carboxylate groups of the CQDs forming strong covalant bonds to a perfect ZnIn2S4 surface was built up as a computational model. The calculation indicated that the total charge of CQDs is −1.02 e, while the sum charges of two carboxylate groups in CQDs is −1.25 e, indicating that a positve charge center with charge of 0.23 e delocalized on the conjugated CQDs. The presence of the delocalized positive and negative charges in Fig. 3C, indicate the formation of FLPs in CQDs, in which the positive and negative charge center would act as Lewis acid and Lewis basic site respectively. To further investigate CQDs' capacity for H2 dissociative adsorption, two hydrogen atoms were then adsorbed in CQDs. The calculated adsorption energy is −0.63 eV with respect to that of the gaseous H2, indicating that CQDs are the adsorption sites for H2 in this system, in consistent with the result of H2-TPD over CQDs, which shows a strong interaction between CQDs and H2 (Fig. S5). The charges of the two adsorbed hydrogen atoms are calculated to be 0.74 and 0.15 e respectively. Considering that the overall charge of CQDs is –1.02 e before H2 adsorption, the presence of two adsorbed hydrogen with different charge indicates that H2 can be polarized to generate H+and H--like species on the CQDs. That is to say, CQDs, with the seperated positive and negative charge centers acting like FLPs, can promote the dissociative adsorption of H2 as noble metal Pd does. In addition, considering that hexagonal ZnIn2S4 consists of a packet stacking of S-Zn-S-In-S-In-S layers, in wihch sulfur vacancies are easily form, models with CQDs deposited on four different ZnIn2S4 surfaces with sulfur vacancies have also used for calculations (Fig. S6-S9 and Tables S1-S2). The results from the vacancy cases were found to be consistent with those from perfect ZnIn2S4, proving the generality of the presence of FLPs in CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposites.

The above theoretical studies clearly indicated that CQDs can act like noble metal Pd to promote the dissociative adsorption of H2, in consistent with the experimental result that the hydrogenation of 3a to targeted 3b occurred over irradiated CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposite in the presence of H2. Therefore, similar to that proposed for N-alkylation of aniline by alcohols over several already reported Pd/photocatalysts [27–30], the mechanism for N-alkylation of aniline over CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposite can be proposed as following (Scheme 1 ). The first step is the photoexcitation of ZnIn2S4 to generate the holes and electrons (Step 1). The photo-generated electrons in ZnIn2S4 can transfer to CQDs to form electron-rich CQDs, due to its more negative conduction band compared with that of CQDs, while the benzyl alcohol react with the hole to form alkoxide intermediate, with released proton transferred to CQDs to protonate the surface carboxylated group (Step 2). The protonization can be evidenced from the in-situ FTIR of the CQDs/ZnIn2S4 during the reaction, which shows the peaks at 1396 cm−1 and 1579 cm−1, assignable to the carboxylate groups of CQDs, significantly weaken, while a new peak at 1711 cm−1, assignable to the carbonyl stretching of the carboxyl emerges (Fig. S10). The CH bond in the alkoxide intermediate further cleaved to led to benzaldehyde form, accompanied by another hydrogen transferred to electron-rich CQDs to form hydride-like CQDs-H with the assistance of photo-generated electrons from excited ZnIn2S4 since there also exist positive charge centers in CQDs, as revealed by the theoretical calculations (Step 3) [43]. Then the Lewis acidic site in the nanocomposites promote the condensation between the aniline and the benzaldehyde to form the intermediate 3a (Step 4). The formation of H+ and H--like species on the surface of CQDs promote the hydrogenation of the as-formed intermediate 3a to generate targeted 3b (Step 5).

The light induced N-alkylation of amines with alcohols over CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposites can also applied to a variety of substrates. The reactions between different amines and alcohols over 3.0 wt% CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposite also led to a variety of N-alkyl amines (Table 2 ). Compared to aniline, anilines bearing electron-donating groups, such as p–CH3 and p–OCH3, afforded product in inferior yields, while anilines bearing electron-withdrawing groups, like p–Cl (99%), exhibited higher yield to corresponding N-alkyl amines. Aliphatic amines and heterocyclic amine were also converted to the corresponding N-alkyl amines. Besides, the N-alkylation of anilines with benzyl alcohols with different substituents are also well tolerated, with yields of 79–86% to the corresponding amines.

In addition, the 3.0 wt% CQDs/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposite showed high stability in the reaction. Three cycling tests reveal no obvious decrease of the activity. The photocatalyst after use shows almost similar XRD pattern to the fresh one (Fig. S11).

The hydrogenation of imines promoted by FLPs in CQDs was demonstrated experimentally and supported theoretically. By coupling of the hydrogenation of imines over CQDs with ZnIn2S4-based photocatalytic dehydrogenation of alcohols, N-alkylation of amines with alcohols were successfully realized over CQDs/ZnIn2S4, with performance comparable to that observed over Pd/ZnIn2S4 nanocomposite. This work demonstrates that CQDs can act like Pd nanoparticles to be a metal-free catalyst for hydrogenation reactions and shows enormous potential of employing CQDs to reduce the current dependence on noble metal-based catalysts in chemical reactions.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by NSFC (21872031, U1705251). Z. Li thanks the Award Program for Minjiang Scholar Professorship for financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcat.2020.01.021.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ligand effects in catalysis by atomically precise gold nanoclusters
Sci. Adv.

Adv. Energy Mater.

Adv. Mater.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphenes in the absence of metals as carbocatalysts for selective acetylene hydrogenation and alkene hydrogenation
